head.ce:title: Synchronously enhanced breakdown strength and energy storage ability of cellulose acetate flexible films via introducing ultra-low content of carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education), Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 610031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education), Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, 610031, China

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Dielectric materials can store electric potential energy under an electric field by inducing an ordered arrangement of molecules and release electric potential energy once the external electric field is turned off or the polarity is changed with the re-arranged charges (Yao et al., 2017). Polymer dielectric materials are promising next-generation energy storage materials, offering various advantages, including easy processing, high power density, high chemical resistance, and long service life. Therefore, they have wide potential applications in many fields (Huang et al., 2023). The main parameters that determine the performances of the dielectric materials are dielectric constant (
ε ′
), dielectric loss ( tanδ ), and breakdown strength (
E b
), which finally determine the energy storage density ( U ). Namely, U = ∫ EdD , where E and D represent the applied electric field and potential shift (Luo et al., 2019; Palumbo et al., 2020), respectively. For the linear dielectric materials, U can also be determined by the following equation: U = 1 / 2
ε 0
ε r
E b 2
, where
ε 0
represents the vacuum dielectric constant (8.85 × 10−12 F/m) and
ε r
is the relative dielectric constant of the dielectric materials, while
E b
is the maximum value of E that can be acted on a dielectric material without inducing loss of insulation (Cai et al., 2019). Thus, to achieve the high U , the dielectric materials are expected to be have high
ε r
and
E b
as much as possible.

At present, some polymers have been commercially used, such as polyimide (PI) (Wu et al., 2023), poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) (Aravinda et al., 2023), and polypropylene (PP) (Zhang et al., 2022), etc. Due to the rigid chain structure feature, PI can be used in the high-temperature condition, and it usually exhibits rather low tanδ , but the
ε r
is also small (Ai et al., 2020). PVDF and PP are polymers with flexible chain structures, and the former has strong polarization ability companioned with high
ε r
but low
E b
, while the latter usually exhibits the inverse scenario, namely, high
E b
but low
ε r
(Ho et al., 2007; Martins et al., 2014). Thus, these commercially used dielectric materials usually exhibit low U . Exploring the new dielectric materials with synchronously enhanced
ε r
and
E b
and reduced tanδ is still challenging. There are mainly two strategies that can achieve the goal. The first one is re-designing the macromolecular structures of polymer matrix, such as introducing the polar groups to enhance the polarization ability (Dong et al., 2024; Gu et al., 2024) or constructing the cross-linked structure to enhance the
E b
(Navarro et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2016). The other strategy is incorporating the dielectric ceramic fillers (Li et al., 2024) or conductive fillers (Wang, Zhang, et al., 2020；Chen, Zhou, et al., 2024). Although the two kinds of fillers can endow the dielectric polymers with high
ε r
, the rising of tanδ and the deterioration of
E b
are hardly to be avoided.

Cellulose and its derivatives have the cyclic chain structures and many oxygen-containing groups in the main chains and generally, there are high density of inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonds in this kind of polymers. Consequently, cellulose-based films usually exhibit ultrahigh mechanical properties, which is conducive to achieving high
E b
theoretically (Goodman et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2023). Furthermore, the polar groups also endow them with relatively high polarization ability. Therefore, from a viewpoint of enhancing U , cellulose and its derivatives have the potential as the dielectric materials (Einfeldt et al., 2001). However, the melt processing and molding of cellulose and its derivatives are relatively difficult since the thermal degradation usually occurs. To date, most of the cellulose-based articles are prepared through solution processing (Crabbe-Mann et al., 2018) with the use of a large quantity of solvents, which brings another problem that needs to be highly noticed, namely, the complete removal of solvent. This is because the residual solvent greatly affects the mechanical and dielectric properties, such as the deterioration of strength and rising of tanδ .

Several strategies have been developed to improve the dielectric properties of cellulose-based materials, including the introduction of additional polar groups such as cyanoethyl groups (Takechi et al., 2016), the incorporation of ceramic fillers such as barium titanate (BaTiO3) (Jia et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2023), alumina (Al2O3) (Yin et al., 2021), and boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS) (Lao et al., 2018), the introduction of semi-conductive particles such as reduced graphene oxide (RGO) (Wang, Wang, & Shao, 2018), titanium dioxide (TiO2) (Madusanka et al., 2016) and polypyrrole (Raghunathan et al., 2017), and the construction of chemical crosslinking structure (Gao et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2018; Zhang, Gao, et al., 2024; Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2024), etc. Among these methods, the introduction of a crosslinking structure has been demonstrated a promising strategy, as it can synchronously improve the mechanical properties and
E b
, endowing the materials with high U . Furthermore, reducing polar groups also weakens the water uptake ability of the materials, which undoubtedly ensures the stability during the long-term service life.

As one of the semi-conductors, quantum dots have been demonstrated to be highly efficient nanoparticles that can tailor the physicochemical properties of matrices (Wang, Huang, et al., 2018). To date, great achievements have already been achieved by researchers in incorporating quantum dots into cellulose to fabricate the advanced functional materials. For instance, Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2016). incorporated CdS quantum dots into functionalized cellulose nanocrystals and fabricated thin films via a layer-by-layer self-assembly technique, and the composite films exhibited high stability and excellent dispersion of CdS. The dispersion of graphene quantum dots within cellulose nanocrystals was also successfully achieved, leading to a marked improvement in the dispersion of the modified cellulose nanocrystals within the nonpolar matrix and consequently, the composites exhibited substantially improved thermal properties (Bajwa et al., 2024). Similarly, the incorporation of polarity-functionalized colloidal quantum dots (QDs) into wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) yielded favorable photostability and elevated photoluminescence (PL) characteristics (Liu, Chowdhury, et al., 2021). Additionally, previous studies have found that incorporating quantum dots into polymers can reduce leakage current density and enhance breakdown strength (Pan et al., 2018) The mechanisms are primarily attributed to the quantum effect known as the Coulomb-blockade effect in quantum dots. Specifically, the tunneling of a second electron can be prevented once the first electron has been injected (Chen, Shi, et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2023). For example, Li et al. (Li et al., 2022) utilized the Coulomb-blockade effect to trap the injected charge and to avoid carrier transport by sputtering gold nanodots in sandwich polymer nanocomposites, and the results showed that the U reached 6.25 J/cm3 at 150 °C with an extremely low filler content of only about 0.0035 vol%. Similarly, Xing et al. (Shuang et al., 2020) found that the addition of small silver nanodots could significantly improve the energy storage density of PI nanocomposite films due to the Coulomb-blockade effect. When the silver content was 0.1 vol%, the energy density of the nanocomposite film reached 5.16 J/cm3 at 600 kV/mm, and the energy efficiency remained at 67.7 %. In comparison to traditional metal-based semiconductor quantum dots, zero-dimensional carbon dots are carbon nanomaterial smaller than 10 nm. They are preferred for their simple synthesis, non-toxicity, good chemical stability, and the controllability of surface functional groups. Carbon dots are divided into three main types: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonated polymer dots (CPDs) (Kang et al., 2022; Farshbaf et al., 2018; 39. Li & Sun, 2022), which have a broad range of application prospects in photoelectric energy devices (El-Shamy, 2019; Xie et al., 2022; Zhu, Ji, et al., 2013). Compared to CQDs and CNDs, CPDs have hybrid structures consisting of a polymer chain shell and a carbon core. On the one hand, the presence of carbon nuclei makes CPDs exhibit a quantum size effect similar to that of inorganic metal-based semiconductor quantum dots. On the other hand, the retention of the polymer chain shell enables CPDs to demonstrate the properties of organic materials and enhances their interfacial bonding with the polymer matrix (Liu, Roy, et al., 2021). It has now become the brightest new star in the industry, surpassing other materials. Similarly, CPDs can be introduced into the polymer matrix as effective nanofillers to enhance its
E b
.

In this study, aiming at developing advanced dielectric materials based on cellulose derivatives, cellulose acetate (CA)/CPDs composite films were fabricated. Compared with the cellulose molecules, a portion of the hydroxyl groups in CA molecules are consumed, increasing the distance between adjacent hydroxyl groups and reducing the hydrogen bonding network. As a result, CA exhibits the characteristics of being less hydrophilic, more soluble, and having a better ability to be used in various applications (Celuppi et al., 2023; Trinh & Mekonnen, 2018). While the residual hydroxyl groups still endow CA with relatively stronger interfacial interaction with other polar particles. Therefore, compared with the natural cellulose, CA is believed to have more potential as the matrix for the dielectric materials. Specifically, CA exhibits lower water-absorbing ability than natural cellulose and hence, it is more conducive to be applied in microelectronic devices. Additionally, CA also exhibits a relatively good film-forming ability. Thus, choosing CA as the matrix for dielectric films is possibly an alternative strategy. Herein, CPDs were synthesized as fillers through a bottom-up hydrothermal amination reaction (Zhang et al., 2015). Considering the possible strong interfacial interaction between CA and CPDs and the wide band gap of CPDs themselves, it was expected that CPDs would have at least dual effects, one was providing sites that would enhance the physical entanglement density of the CA matrix, enhancing mechanical properties and mechanical breakdown tolerance; the other was capturing carriers and inhibiting their migration in the matrix through the so-called Coulomb-blockade effect, thereby suppressing the generation of leakage currents. Thus, it was also expected that the incorporation of a few amounts of CPDs would enhance the breakdown strength and energy storage properties of the composite films. Here, a series of CA/CPDs composite films with different contents of CPDs were fabricated to thoroughly investigate the role of CPDs in tailoring the dielectric and ferroelectric properties, as well as the energy storage capacity and dielectric breakdown behaviors of the composite films. Interestingly, the results showed that the transparent and flexible composite film with only 0.1 wt% CPDs had 1.62 times higher
E b
(520.28 MV/m) than the pure CA film (321.94 MV/m), and the discharging energy density reached 2.55 J/cm3 at 450 MV/m, with an efficiency of 73.3 %. This performance is comparable to the commercially used polypropylene films (Qi et al., 2014), and it can serve as an alternative in the next generation of energy storage materials to satisfy sustainability requirements.

CA was provided by Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD (Mn ∼ 30,000 g/mol, DS = 2.45, 39.8 wt% acetyl, and 3.5 wt% hydroxy content, Shanghai, China). Acetone, urea, anhydrous citric acid and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9 %) were supplied by Kelong Chemical Co (Chengdu, China), and all raw materials and reagents were used directly. Deionized water was prepared in the laboratory.

CPDs were prepared through a bottom-up hydrothermal method, and the synthesis routine is schematically represented in Fig. 1 . First, citric acid (1.26 g) and urea (1.08 g) were mixed with deionized water (30 mL) and then transferred into a reactor (with cubage of 50 mL). The mixture was heated in an oven set at 200 °C for 6 h, followed by cooling the oven to 23 °C. The brown solution was then centrifuged at least 3 times at 1200 rpm for 15 min. After each centrifugation, the supernatant was removed and the process continued until no precipitate remained. The solution was then placed in a dialysis bag and dialyzed for 48 h. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to obtain CPDs powders. The experiment was repeated for preparation at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CA/CPDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The CA/CPDs dielectric films were obtained through a scraping technique as shown in Fig. 1 too. First, a mixed solvent of acetone and DMF was prepared with a volume fraction ratio of 2:1. CPDs powders were added, followed by the addition of CA. The mass fraction of CA was maintained at 6 wt% relative to the mass of the mixed solvents. After being treated and dispersed for 2 h under the sonication condition, the solution was further magnetically stirred for 24 h and then vacuum-defoamed for 30 min. A 500-μm squeegee was used to produce a homogeneous film on a glass substrate. The prepared solution was evenly scraped onto the substrate and then the substrate was transferred into a vacuum oven. After drying at 80 °C for 12 h, the film was carefully peeled off to obtain the dielectric film for microstructure characterization and performance measurements. A series of composite films with different CPDs contents were fabricated, and the CPDs contents were set at 0.05 wt% (CA/CPDs-0.05), 0.1 wt% (CA/CPDs-0.1), 0.2 wt% (CA/CPDs-0.2), 0.5 wt% (CA/CPDs-0.5), and 1 wt% (CA/CPDs-1), respectively. For comparison, the pure CA film was also prepared through the completely same fabrication procedures, just without adding CPDs. At least four batches of thin films were prepared for testing.

The functional groups of components were identified by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS20, Thermo Fisher). A UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600i Plus, Shimadzu) was used to test the transmission spectra of the CA/CPDs films and the absorption spectra of the CPDs. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-F200, JEOL) operated at 200 kV was used to observe the lattice fringes of CPDs and the dispersion of CPDs in the composite films. Before the test, the CPDs dispersion was ultrasonically treated for 1 h and then dropped onto the copper grid. Furthermore, to observe the dispersion state of CPDs in the dielectric film, the film was first embedded in epoxy resin, and then the ultrathin slices were obtained using a cryoultramicrotome. In addition, the particle size of CPDs was observed by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8, Bruker) in contact mode. Before the test, the CPDs dispersion liquid was dried on a clean silicon wafer. The cross-sectional morphologies of the CA/CPDs composite films were observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7800F, JEOL). Before the measurement, a layer of gold with a thickness of approximately 10 nm was sputtered on the sample surface for SEM characterization. The crystalline structures of the pure CA and CA/CPDs films were examined using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker) with Cu Kα radiation, and it was operated at 45 kV and 40 mA. Further structural and composition analysis of samples were performed using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscope (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific). Vantage software was used to analyze the data. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Netzsch) was used to analyze the thermal stability of the pure CA and CA/CPDs composite films. For the analysis, 5–10 mg of the sample was heated from 30 °C to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The dielectric properties were investigated at room temperature using a broad frequency dielectric spectrograph (BDS, Concept 41, Novocontrol) at 220 V and with a frequency range of 101–106 Hz. Before BDS testing, gold electrodes were fabricated by sputtering treatment, and the radius of the electrodes was 2 mm. Each sample was subjected to a minimum of five tests to ensure the reliability of the results. The breakdown strength was conducted by a breakdown voltage tester (BDJC-50, Beikou Economic Instruments). Each sample was subjected to at least 15 tests, and 10 points were selected for plotting to ensure the reliability of the results. The hysteresis loop was measured at a frequency of 100 Hz and a voltage range of 0–10 kV using a ferroelectric test system (TF2000E, aixACCT). The current density measurement was also performed on this instrument with the same film sample. The results presented are the means of at least five repetitive tests. The mechanical properties were measured at room temperature of 25 °C and a humidity of 55 % through using a universal tensile machine (UTM4104X, Suns) at a cross-head speed of 5 mm/min. The rectangular sample with a length of 3 cm, a width of 1.5 cm and a thickness of 60 μm was directly cut from the film as prepared. The reported data for each composition were derived from at least five specimens, and the average data were reported and analyzed.

The microstructure and physicochemical properties of CPDs were first characterized. Fig. 2(a) and 2(b) show the typical HR-TEM images. All CPDs are spherical as prepared. Some lattice fringes can be differentiated, with lattice spacings of 0.21 and 0.31 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of graphene (Wang et al., 2021; Wang, Zhou, et al., 2022), respectively. This indicates that the prepared CPDs have a graphene-like structure. To further determine the particle size, especially the diameter distribution of CPDs, AFM characterization was performed at a relatively lower magnification. Fig. 2(c) displays the AFM amplitude image, indicating that the CPDs are relatively uniform in size, with an average diameter of about 5.13 ± 0.02 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(d), which is consistent with the particle size reported in the literatures (1–10 nm, Table S1). (Xia et al., 2019). Generally, CPDs exhibit distinct fluorescence phenomena. As shown in the inset photo in Fig. 2(c), the CPDs solution appears light brown under natural light and blue when exposed to UV irradiation. The uniform fluorescence also confirms the good dispersion stability of CPDs in solution.

The microstructure of CPDs was further characterized by XRD. As seen in Fig. 3(a)，CPDs exhibit a clear diffuse peak at 2θ of 20–30°, confirming that the CPDs mainly exhibit the amorphous state (Han et al., 2024). Furthermore, FTIR, UV–Vis, and XPS were used to characterize the surface chemical characteristics of CPDs. From Fig. 3(b), the characteristic absorption peaks at 1403.9 and 3425.4 cm−1 are related to the OH bending and stretching vibration of the OH bond, respectively, while those absorption peaks at 3151.1 and 1577.5 cm−1 can be ascribed to the NH and CN bonds stretching vibrations (Li et al., 2023), respectively. This indicates that the surface of CPDs contains a large quantity of hydroxyl and amino functional groups. Similar results have also been reported in the literature (Lai et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and microstructure of the CA/CPDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology analysis of the CA/CPDs composite films was conducted on SEM. Fig. 4 (a1) and 4(b1) show the surface morphologies of the pure CA film and CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film, respectively, while the cross-sectional morphologies are displayed in Fig. 4(a2, a3) and Fig. 4(b2, b3), respectively. It is evident that both the pure CA and CA/CPDs-0.1 films have smooth surfaces with no significant difference between them. Furthermore, the cross-sections of the films are defect-free and relatively dense, indicating that the addition of CPDs does not affect film quality during the preparation process. The typical TEM image of the CA/CPDs-0.5 composite film is shown in Fig. 4(c). It is clearly seen that CPDs exhibit homogeneous dispersion in the CA matrix without apparent aggregation. Furthermore, the composite film also demonstrates good flexibility, as shown in the inserted digital photograph in Fig. 4(c). UV–vis tests were further conducted to evaluate the transparency of the composite films, using the visible light transmittance at a wavelength of 550 nm as the evaluation criteria. Seen in Fig. 4(d), With the increase in CPDs content, light transmittance decreases slightly. However, even with the CA/CPDs-1 composite film, which has the highest CPDs content, the transmittance remains as high as 89.5 %, which indicates that the films maintain good transparency after the introduction of CPDs. Comparative analysis shown in Fig. 4(e) further indicates that the CA/CPDs-1 composite film has a higher light transmittance than many other materials. (De Moraes et al., 2015; Devarayan et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2022; Rafiqul Bari & Kim, 2019; Wang, Yin, et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021) The good dispersion of CPDs, confirmed by the high transmittance of the composite films, suggests greater application potential. Additionally, it is worth noting that as the CPDs content increases, the composite films exhibit UV absorption in the UV region. This also suggests a potential UV shielding function for the composite films, which will be further studied in the future. As shown in digital photo in Fig. 4(d), the CA/CPDs-0.5 film emits uniform blue light under the irradiation of a UV lamp with a wavelength of 356 nm, further demonstrating the homogeneous dispersion of CPDs in the composite film.

Fig. 5(a) represents the FTIR spectra of pure CA and CA/CPDs composite films in the 4000–500 cm−1 range. The absorption peak of CA at 3474 cm−1 is related to -OH stretching vibration, while the absorption peaks at 1732.2 and 1218.8 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO and CO stretching vibrations of the acetyl groups, respectively (Borges et al., 2023). Comparing CA and CA/CPDs-1 films, the absorption peak of the -OH groups moves within a small range from 3473.1 cm−1 to 3467.5 cm−1, confirming the hydrogen bonding interaction between CPDs (amino groups) and CA (hydroxyl groups) (Battirola et al., 2018; Vinodhini et al., 2017). However, the amino stretching vibration peak is not observed in the FTIR spectra of the composite films, possibly due that the fact that the CPDs content in the composite is too low and the absorption peaks of amino and hydroxyl groups merge into one peak. The impact of CPDs on the thermal stability of the composite films was also investigated. According to Fig. S1, the thermal stability of the composite films is not significantly affected. The results obtained from XRD characterization further confirm that the crystalline structure of CA is not influenced by CPDs (Fig. S2).

For a better understanding of the role of CPDs in the composite films, the interaction between components in the represented composite films was further investigated by XPS characterization. For comparison, the results of the pure CA film are also provided. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the pure CA film exhibits peaks at binding energies of 286.91 eV (C 1 s) and 533.22 eV (O 1 s), with relative fractions of 60.71 % and 39.29 %, respectively. Similarly, the CA/CPDs-0.1 film shows peaks at 286.34 eV (C 1 s) and 533.11 eV (O 1 s), with relative fractions of 62.75 % and 37.25 %, respectively. While, the CA/CPDs-1 composite film exhibits peaks at 285.86 eV (C 1 s) and 533.03 eV (O 1 s), with relative fractions of 68.72 % and 28.84 %. Furthermore, the CA/CPDs-1 composite film also shows a weak peak of N 1 s at 400.12 eV with a relative fraction of 2.04 %. The presence of N 1 s peak can be mainly ascribed to the amino groups on the CPDs surface. For the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film, the N 1 s peak is not present, likely because of the relatively low content of CPDs, and therefore, this peak cannot be detected by XPS characterization. From the high-resolution of C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s (Fig. 5(c-e)), it can be seen that the binding energies related to the C-O-C/C-O-H, O-C=O/O-C-O, CO, C-O-C and C-O-H bonds tend to increase with increasing CPDs content, while the binding energies of C-C/C-H maintains invariant. This indicates that there is an interaction between the CA molecules and the CPDs surfaces, and such interaction becomes stronger at high CPDs content (Zhang et al., 2023). The observations obtained through XPS characterization agree well with those obtained through FTIR measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dielectric properties of CA/CPDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The variations in
ε r
and tanδ of the samples are shown in Fig. 6(b, c), and the data obtained at 102 Hz are compared in Fig. 6(d). Generally, the
ε r
of polymers is mainly affected by the motion of the chain segments, the activity of the dipoles, and the polarity of macromolecules (Islam et al., 2023). For composites, the presence of interfaces brings the interfacial polarization, which is conducive to the enhancement of
ε r
. In this work, the pure CA sample shows a
ε r
of 7.24 and a tanδ of 0.025, respectively. The relatively high
ε r
may be attributed to the strong polarization ability of the polar groups that do not participate in hydrogen bond formation. For the composite films,
ε r
decreases initially, with the lowest
ε r
achieved for the CA/CPDs-0.1 sample, and then it gradually increases with further increasing CPDs content. However, even when the CPDs content reaches 1 wt%, the
ε r
remains relatively low (7.35), very close to that of the pure CA sample. CPDs do not show the reinforcement effect in tailoring the
ε r
of the composite films possibly due to that the content of CPDs is relatively small. Furtherly, all the composite films show the similar tanδ compared with the pure CA film. The mechanisms for the variations of
ε r
and tanδ will be further discussed in the following.

E b
is another critical indicator of dielectric composite films in dielectric applications. Herein, the Weibull distribution of
E b
and the characteristic
E b
are calculated using the following equation (Pan et al., 2016):
(1)
P
E
= 1 − exp
−
E
E b
β

In this context, P
E
represents the cumulative probability of electric breakdown, while E and
E b
represent the measured and characteristic breakdown strength, respectively, and β represents the reliability parameter. Fig. 7(a) displays the Weibull distribution of all samples, and the corresponding
E b
values are illustrated in Fig. 7(b). The pure CA film shows the
E b
value of 321.94 MV/m. Enhanced
E b
are achieved for all the CA/CPDs composite films. The
E b
gradually increases with increasing CPDs content and the maximum value (520.58 MV/m) is achieved for the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film, then
E b
decreases with further increasing CPDs content. Although the CA/CPDs-1 composite film shows the smallest
E b
(396.03 MV/m) among composite films, the
E b
remains higher than that of the pure CA film. Furthermore, the reproducibility of the data is good, as indicated by β values all being greater than 10. For better understanding the advantage of the characteristic breakdown strength of the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film, a simple comparison on breakdown strength is made between CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film and other dielectric films reported in literatures. As shown in Fig. S3, among these dielectric films, the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film exhibits the highest
E b
, confirming that introducing CPDs into CA is the efficient strategy to achieving high breakdown strength, which is favorable for the potential application of such composite films.

The current densities of the samples were then measured, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 8(a). It is clear that the presence of CPDs leads to a reduction in current density, especially at relatively high electric fields, which can be attributed to the Coulomb-blockade effect of CPDs (Wang, Gou, et al., 2022). By measuring the D − E loops shown in Fig. 8(b), the charge/discharge behavior of different samples under high electric fields were investigated. Compared with the pure CA film, the composite films show smaller residual polarization, and the D − E loops also become slimmer. Generally, the net polarization parameter that is defined by
D m
−
D r
, in which D
m and D
r are the maximum and residual polarization, respectively, can be used to judge the polarization ability of the dielectric composites. Seen in Fig. 8(c), the pure CA sample shows a net polarization parameter of 1.10 μC/cm2. While for the composite films, most of them exhibit lower net polarization parameters, except the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film that exhibits the maximum net polarization parameter of 1.20 μC/cm2. This further confirms that the contribution of CPDs in enhancing the polarization ability of the composites is relatively small. According to the D − E loops of each film obtained at different electric fields as shown in Fig. S4, the discharging energy density (
U d
) and the energy efficiency ( η ) were calculated (Luo et al., 2017), and the results are illustrated in Fig. 8(d). As the electric field increases, the
U d
of the composite films increases too, but the leakage current and hysteresis loss lead to the reduction of η. Among these samples, the
U d
of CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film at 450 MV/m achieves 2.55 J/cm3, which is 1.36 times higher than the
U d
(1.87 J/cm3) of the pure CA film at 400 MV/m. Additionally, the η still remains at ∼73.3 % at this time. The good energy storage ability of the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film is comparable to that of the commercial biaxially-oriented polypropylene (BOPP) film (Han et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CA/CPDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The good mechanical properties are a prerequisite for the engineering application of dielectric films. Furthermore, understanding the reinforcement mechanisms in mechanical properties can indirectly explain the enhancement of
E b
. Here, the tensile properties of the samples were measured. Fig. 9(a) and 9(b) display the typical engineering stress-strain plots of the CA and CA/CPDs films and the corresponding mechanical performance parameters, respectively. Compared with the pure CA film, which exhibits relatively low tensile strength (22.75 MPa) and ductility (1.076 %), the composite films achieve significantly enhanced tensile strength and ductility. And among these composite films, the CA/CPDs-0.1 sample shows the best mechanical properties, with a tensile strength of 60.35 MPa and an elongation at break of 3.09 %. While further increasing the content of CPDs leads to the deterioration of the mechanical properties. However, it is noticed that the CA/CPDs-1 sample still shows better mechanical properties compared with the pure CA. Moreover, incorporating small amounts of CPDs is conductive to the improvement of the CA films. The mechanisms are possibly related to the enhanced local entanglement density of CA matrix, which increases the probability of covalent bond fracture mode, thus leading to higher tensile strength and ductility on one hand. On the other hand, with the presence of a few CPDs in the composite films, the film-forming ability and quality of films are possibly improved, reducing the defects in films and thus leading to better mechanical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Further understanding about the role of CPDs in composite film
ce:section.ce:para:
The above results clearly confirm that introducing CPDs leads to the enhancement of
E b
and mechanical properties of CA, while the
ε r
and tanδ have little change. Most importantly, the composite films show a largely enhanced discharging energy density with high efficiency. To better understand the role of CPDs in the CA composite films, more characterization and measurements were carried out. First, the absorption values of pure CA and CA/CPDs-0.1 films were measured using the UV–vis spectrometer, and the band gap values were calculated with the formula (Note S1). As shown in Fig. 10 , the band gap of the CA/CPDs-0.1 composite film increases from 5.58 eV of the pure CA film to 5.65 eV. An increase in the band gap indicates that electron transition in the valence band becomes more difficult in the CA composite film. In other words, the composite film has more or strengthened traps that can more effectively capture or bind the carriers. As a result, charge accumulation and conduction in the composite films are inhibited, thus increasing the breakdown strength (Cai et al., 2023; Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2024). Simultaneously, the distinctive Coulomb-blockade effect of CPDs can effectively hinder carrier migration, thereby inhibiting electron conduction and enhancing the breakdown strength. This can be directly confirmed through the reduced leakage current of the composite films as indicated in Fig. 8(a). However, as the packing density of CPDs increases, the distance between them decreases, which makes electron conduction easier. Specifically, an increase in CPDs content also leads to more defects, enhancing the probability of breakdown in the electrical field.

Based on the above results, the role of CPDs in tailoring the energy storage ability and mechanical properties of CA can be explained through the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 11 . First, the polar groups on the surface of the CPDs and the molecular chains of CA exhibit strong interfacial interactions, which lead to the enhancement of local entanglement density of the molecular chains. On the one hand, the enhanced entanglement density weakens the directional alignment of the dipoles under the electric field, limits the movement of charge carriers, and reduces the leakage current, which results in a low polarization ability companioned by a low
ε r
and tanδ but enhanced
E b
. On the other hand, the enhanced local entanglement density also endows the composite films with high mechanical properties, as it increases the probability of covalent bond fracture during the tensile process. Furthermore, the strong interfacial interaction may promote stress transferring between CPDs and CA matrix, which is also conducive to the enhancement of the mechanical properties.

In summary, CPDs with polar groups on surface have been successfully fabricated through a bottom-up hydrothermal method. After being incorporated into CA, the CA/CPDs composite films exhibit homogeneous dispersion of CPDs, good transparency and strong interfacial interaction between CPDs and CA matrix. Dielectric properties measurements indicate that the composite films have a lower leakage current density than the pure CA films, a higher dielectric strength of 520.58 MV/m, and simultaneously, the dielectric loss is still maintained at a low level. At the CPDs content of only 0.1 wt%, the composite film shows the highest
U d
of 2.55 J/cm3 at 450 MV/m, which is 1.36 times that of the pure CA film (1.87 J/cm3) at 400 MV/m, and currently, the η still maintains at 73.3 %. Mechanical properties measurements show that CPDs exhibit reinforcement effect in the composite films, leading to the synchronously intensified tensile strength and ductility compared with the pure CA film. The special Coulomb-blockade effect of CPDs and the enhanced local entanglement density of CA matrix induced by CPDs are suggested the main mechanisms for the enhancement of the energy storage ability and mechanical properties. This research confirms that incorporating CPDs into cellulose-based derivatives is possibly promising way to fabricate dielectric materials for energy storage and application and is worthy of further study in the future.
